note.soap:
S:  
34-year-old adult presents by phone reporting a tick bite sustained in Georgia approximately 37 hours ago. Patient describes an expanding bullseye-like rash consistent with erythema migrans at the site of the tick bite. No systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, or body aches reported. No medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant.

O:  
No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Patient describes a characteristic bullseye rash consistent with erythema migrans at the tick bite site. No reported systemic signs or symptoms.

A:  
 - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease  
 - Differential:  
  1. Early localized Lyme disease  
  2. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)  
  3. Cellulitis or local skin infection  
  4. Allergic reaction to tick bite  
 - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms reported (fever, chills, severe headache, body aches, stiff neck).

P:  
 - Disposition: Outpatient management with close follow-up if symptoms worsen or systemic signs develop.  
 - Plan: Recommended regimen; reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated: doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 10 days. Alternatives if doxycycline contraindicated or not tolerated include amoxicillin 500 mg PO three times daily for 14 days or cefuroxime axetil 500 mg PO twice daily for 14 days. Azithromycin 500 mg PO daily for 7 days is a less effective alternative if other antibiotics cannot be used.  
 - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if fever, worsening rash, new systemic symptoms, or neurologic symptoms develop. Follow up with primary care or urgent care if symptoms do not improve or worsen within 48-72 hours.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
